data.name: Hernán Galíndez
data.description: After being released as a youngster by Rosario Central, his hometown club in Argentina, Galíndez rebuilt his life in Quito and embraced Ecuador as his new home and identity. Now, with more than 300 domestic matches played and unforgettable moments such as sealing World Cup qualification against Argentina, he stands as a national star, but he doesn’t like to boast. “I’m not a superhero, I’m just a football player and nothing more,” he says. As a seven-year-old in Rosario, testing himself out as a keeper for the first time after the regular No 1 was injured, he came up against a lively young rival. “I played in this tournament and only conceded one goal. To this day, my mom has a plaque from that day. That goal was scored by a certain Lionel Messi.” Now, at 38, Galíndez is a figurehead for the side.

data.name: Félix Torres
data.description: Torres surprised everyone with his progress. A skinny boy from Esmeraldas, he didn’t look like an obvious future dominant international centre-back. But Daniel Peña, a club coach on a scouting mission in 2015 to check a different player training for Alianza del Pailón, noticed Torres instead and signed him up. Peña reflected in 2023: “This boy had good ball control, good positioning. He made difficult things look easy. The downside was that he didn't know how to head the ball and he was very skinny. But these days his physique and his heading are his greatest strengths.” A proper leader, he made his international debut in 2019 and brings maturity and work ethic to the backline.

data.name: Piero Hincapié
data.description: One of the most complete Ecuadorian defenders of his generation, known for tactical intelligence, clean ball distribution and versatility as a centre-back and left-back. Educated in the Independiente del Valle youth system, he moved to Talleres before shining at Bayer Leverkusen, and joined Arsenal on an initial loan deal last year. “It only took one conversation to make me feel sure about the decision that I was taking to come here. I'm over the moon …  I want to win trophies for this club, to make a name for myself, and to leave a mark.” A full international from a young age, he has already played in a World Cup and the Copa América, and is developing into a real leader at the back.

data.name: Joel Ordóñez
data.description: Ordóñez is a towering centre-back who has become a pillar at Club Brugge, known for his maturity and defensive intelligence. Born in Guayaquil and raised in the Independiente del Valle youth system, he moved to Belgium in 2022 and steadily built his reputation, growing from a raw prospect into a reliable starter and international option for Ecuador. His rise reflects focus, patience, and consistency. One of his early coaches said it could have been a different career trajectory, though, recalling his boyhood desire to be the star man up front, to “be Enner Valencia … to be our Balotelli”. His future at the back, though, looks extremely bright at club and international level. He has the mentality to become one of the top Ecuadorian defenders of his generation, and has been repeatedly linked with a Premier League move.

data.name: Jordy Alcívar
data.description: A balanced and intelligent midfielder, Alcívar has become a key figure at Independiente del Valle as a central pivot and organiser. Born in Manta, he stands out for his defensive solidity, clean ball distribution, tactical discipline and versatility across the line. Developed at LDU Quito, where he was known by the nickname La Bochorna 44 (“We played Free Fire [a shooter game] online and I needed a nickname. I chose that one and they’ve been calling me that ever since”), he gained international exposure with Charlotte FC in MLS before returning to Ecuador in 2023, where he has since lifted major trophies, including the 2025 Serie A and Copa Ecuador. A full international since 2021.

data.name: Willian Pacho
data.description: From the humble streets of Quinindé, Pacho rose meteorically from Independiente del Valle to conquer Europe, becoming the first Ecuadorian to win the Champions League with Paris Saint-Germain's historic 5-0 thrashing of Inter last year. He’s known for being humble and respectful, qualities he says were implanted early on. “My mum always instilled in me that you have to respect people always, be kind on and off the pitch … I've always said that, above all things, you have to respect everyone.” She died when she was 51 and his No 51 shirt honours her memory. Also watch out for his trademark wrist bandage – a lucky charm from a hand injury sustained at Antwerp. “I fractured my hand, then I had to bandage it, and right after I scored a goal against Australia. Since then I’ve always used the bandage, because it feels good.” His first senior Ecuador call-up shocked him, as he had not made any appearances at youth level. “I went straight to the first senior call, it was incredible, I didn't expect it … I called my family, told them and they all screamed, they were so happy.” Similarly, when he joined PSG from Eintracht Frankfurt in 2024: “I called my sisters, and they cried a lot.”

data.name: Pervis Estupiñán
data.description: The explosive left-back arrived at Milan last year in a landmark deal, becoming the first Ecuadorian to wear the Rossoneri shirt. It has been quite a journey. As a boy he combined football with helping on his mum’s meatball stand selling empanadas. His football career began properly at LDU Quito in 2015, taking him via Watford, Granada, Almería, Mallorca, Osasuna, Villarreal and Brighton before arriving at San Siro. Speaking at his unveiling he said he had “received so many messages from Ecuador. Everyone is claiming they’ve been Milan fans since birth, just because I’m here. I just hope they all buy the shirt.” His early club performances drew criticism, but even under pressure Estupiñán remains mentally strong. He brings plenty of knowhow to this summer’s squad.

data.name: Anthony Valencia
data.description: Like Jackson Porozo, Valencia did his chances of being included in the final 26-man squad no harm by finding the net in Ecuador’s friendly victory against Saudi Arabia the day before the announcement. The 22-year-old showed his striker’s instinct to score his first international goal, prodding a first-time shot past the goalkeeper in New Jersey. In a post-match interview Valencia could not stop smiling and thanked his mother watching at home. “She’s not here but I’m sure she’s very happy,” he said. The attacker has spent almost his entire senior career at Royal Antwerp, playing a bit-part role as the club won their first Belgian league title in 66 years in 2023. Not a prolific goalscorer by any means, Valencia is comfortable playing behind the main striker or even as a right wing-back.

data.name: John Yeboah
data.description: A versatile forward, Yeboah was born in Hamburg and was eligible for Germany and, through his parents, Ghana and Ecuador. He played at youth level for Germany from the under-16s to the under-20s, but ultimately chose Ecuador in 2023. Comfortable as a winger, second striker or attacking midfielder, he is known for agility, close control, sharp movement between lines, and the ability to unbalance defenders in one-on-one situations. His career path through Germany, the Netherlands, Poland and now Italy with Venezia has shaped a resilient, adaptable footballer with a strong mentality. Talking in January about his decision to choose Ecuador he said: “Germany is a nation that has given me so many opportunities both in football and in life, but my heart and soul obviously belong to Ecuador. I'm ready to give my all for the national team.”

data.name: Kendry Páez
data.description: Ecuador’s generational talent, a left-footed prodigy full of creativity and fearlessness. Developed at Independiente del Valle, he made his debut in the top flight at just 15 and quickly drew global interest; Borussia Dortmund and Manchester United were linked before he agreed a £17m move to Chelsea in 2023. After a development spell in France with Strasbourg he joined River Plate this year. He has been a senior international since September 2023 when he made his debut aged 16 years and 131 days in a World Cup qualifier against Uruguay – 11 days short of beating Diego Maradona’s record of being the youngest male player to play for a South American national team. A month later he scored against Bolivia, becoming the youngest ever Conmebol World Cup qualifiers goalscorer. In January, Chelsea's then coach, Liam Rosenior, called him “a kid who has a gift … He's an ultra-talented player,” while his teammate Moisés Caicedo said: “Everyone knows that he is great and if he does his part and dedicates himself every day, in training and outside, he will be a total great. The talent he has is incredible."

data.name: Kevin Rodríguez
data.description: Emerging from Imbabura in Ecuador’s Serie B, Rodríguez surprised fans with his rapid rise, his call-up to the 2022 World Cup squad as a second-tier player being heavily criticised. But that marked a gear shift in his career: soon afterwards he signed for Independiente del Valle and made a quick impression on overseas scouts with his height and strength. Known as La Rola, he moved to Belgium with Union Saint-Gilloise in 2023, making his debut in a Europa League group game against Toulouse. He struggled for goals at first, but hit form just at the right time to secure his place in this summer’s World Cup squad.

data.name: Moisés Ramírez
data.description: With sharp reflexes, calm distribution and a strong mentality, Ramírez made a quick start to his career, winning major titles and national call-ups at a young age. But his early career wasn’t just defined by trophies; he was also shaped by pressure and criticism after some high-profile mistakes, forcing him to grow up fast. His overseas loan from Independiente del Valle to Real Sociedad in 2019 helped him develop and become more resilient. He said: “The criticism made me stronger personally, I matured and made better decisions.” He now plays his club football in Greece with AE Kifisia.

data.name: Enner Valencia
data.description: Known as Superman for a reason. His nation’s all-time leading scorer came into this tournament having scored a record six World Cup goals for Ecuador, and is still going strong at 36 after rejoining Pachuca, where he once lifted the Liga MX Golden Boot. From sleeping in Emelec's stadium as a kid and raising the money for his first pair of boots by milking cows on his dad’s farm to shining at West Ham, Everton, Tigres, Fenerbahce, Internacional, and now back in Mexico, his journey has been full of drama, and a little chaos, too. During a notorious 2016 World Cup qualifier against Chile in Quito he was playing with an arrest warrant looming over him due to a dispute over child maintenance payments, and noticed police loitering on the touchline to grab him at the final whistle. So, with 82 minutes on the clock, he collapsed, was taken off on a stretcher wearing an oxygen mask and rushed to an ambulance, which was then chased by the group of police officers around the running track. He escaped to a clinic; the dispute was later resolved.

data.name: Alan Minda
data.description: An explosive winger, Minda is a product of the Independiente del Valle academy, rising through youth football with early experiences in LigaPro, the Copa Libertadores, a South American Under-20 Championship, and an Under-20 World Cup, on the way to becoming one of Ecuador’s brightest attacking hopes. Known for his dribbling, acceleration and ability to break defensive lines, he found the net in the Copa América and World Cup qualifiers. Now continuing his development at Atlético Mineiro after signing a five-year deal in January, Minda is fearless and decisive in transitions. He said previously his mindset is to “have full confidence, and that I shouldn’t be afraid to make mistakes … I follow the dream I’ve had since I was a kid: to play, move overseas, and help my family.”

data.name: Pedro Vite
data.description: A talented left-footed creator. He made his competitive debut in 2019 in Serie B with Independiente del Valle’s reserves, Independiente Juniors, before his senior Serie A debut in 2021. In August that year, aged 19, he made the leap to MLS with Vancouver Whitecaps, where his progress – in terms of consistency, maturity and leadership – was rapid. Last year he moved to Mexico with Pumas Unam. He has spoken openly about his struggles with homesickness as a youngster, and about mental health more broadly. “When I went to Quito, to Independiente, it was difficult because I was very close to my mum. I left when I was 12, and I cried until I was 14 or 15. I couldn't adapt to Quito. I liked my teammates, but I missed being with my mum. Our voices would break when we could finally speak! I suffered, and I've had a psychologist since I was 12. People need to change how they see that in their minds; they think that if you have one it's because you're unwell or crazy, but a psychologist helps you grow as a person."

data.name: Jordy Caicedo
data.description: A classic No 9, Caicedo brings power, presence, a scoring instinct and strong heading ability. In 2019 he spoke about his first steps in football, growing up in the humble neighbourhood of Nuevo Pilo in Machala. “It was economically poor there, but there were many boys and we didn't see the poverty. We used to play street football in neighborhoods with rich kids, we bet money and we always won. We were the best. We used the money to buy bread and soda.” When he wasn’t playing he was helping his dad keep the family afloat. “My father sold coconut and tamarind juice, as well as coconut candy, on the street and on the beach, and I helped him." That stopped when, at 13, Caicedo was scouted at his local club. At 17 he turned professional with Universidad Católica de Quito. His career since has taken in spells in Brazil, Bulgaria, Mexico, Turkey and Spain, and he moved to Argentina with Huracán last year.

data.name: Ángelo Preciado
data.description: Preciado, raised in the Independiente del Valle academy, came through a difficult childhood to make it this far. When he was 16, his father, who had closely guided his son’s development, was jailed. The shock made Preciado even more determined, and he has since grown into a key figure for Ecuador. He helped secure his dad’s freedom, and told him: “Now you can see that little boy you had by your side taking part in the greatest tournament in the world.” He may be best known to some for an incident in February 2018 when he went viral online after TV footage showed him using a corner flag to defend himself during a mass brawl in the Under-20 Copa Libertadores.

data.name: Denil Castillo
data.description: A resilient presence in the heart of midfield, Castillo has developed hugely since moving to Denmark with Midtjylland in 2024, emerging as one of the club’s most promising young prospects. He left home at a very young age to chase his dream of making it in professional football, with his career path taking him from Ukraine’s Shakhtar Donetsk during wartime conditions to a brief spell at Partizan Belgrade on the way to Denmark, where he says he feels safe, settled and confident again. Calm on the ball, tactically intelligent, and mentally strong, Castillo carries his story with pride, recalling his time in Ukraine: “You could hear the bombs every day, but I had to stay focused and keep going.”

data.name: Gonzalo Plata
data.description: An explosive wide player who can be a real threat from the right wing or as an inside-forward. He started out at Independiente del Valle, where he credits the then coach, Juan Carlos León, with changing his mindset. “I was very argumentative as a child, I would get annoyed by everything on the field. I would get sent off because the opposing players would hit me or insult me, and I would fall for their provocation. I was also very lazy, I didn't like to train much, I didn't like to run, I didn't like the physical aspect. He made me change that way of being, that way of playing, to put much more pressure on myself.” His club career journey has taken him via Portugal’s Sporting, Spain’s Real Valladolid and Qatar’s Al Sadd to Brazil with Flamengo since 2024. It hasn’t always been smooth progress, though: in 2021 Valladolid issued a statement “deploring” his behaviour after he was involved in an early-hours traffic accident. Plata said: “There’s no justification for what happened. It was a bad act on my part. I let myself get carried away with nightlife.”

data.name: Nilson Angulo
data.description: Angulo’s senior career started in 2021 with LDU Quito, where his talent quickly drew attention from overseas scouts. He moved to Belgium’s Anderlecht in 2022 on a five-year deal, and his consistently productive displays in their first team earned links with a host of Premier League clubs before Sunderland signed him for £17.5m in the last winter window. The club’s then sporting director, Kristjaan Speakman, called Angulo “an incredibly exciting prospect, quick and creative, [who] excels in all the core actions required to impact the final third”. Angulo himself said: “My development as a player in Anderlecht was great, but I hope to keep improving even more in Sunderland. I believe in my abilities.” He grew up watching Neymar skills videos. “My idol is Neymar. When I played with my friends, I always tried to imitate him.”

data.name: Alan Franco
data.description: A strong, versatile midfield operator, another who came through the youth system at Independiente del Valle. His career path has been shaped not only by football, but by personal loss: his father, Pedro Franco, died during the pandemic in 2020, just days before Alan signed for Atlético Mineiro. He has dedicated subsequent trophies and goals to his father. An adaptable performer, he was tipped as a future striker by one of his youth coaches, but has been deployed in defence by the national team. “I can play in any position I have to,” he said last year. “I'm a midfielder, I'm a full-back. Things change; in football, you have to be multifunctional. It doesn't bother me; it's something I like, and it's a positive thing for me to adapt.”

data.name: Gonzalo Valle
data.description: Valle has established himself as a reliable, mentally strong keeper who gained continental recognition with a huge performance in the 2025 Copa Libertadores, making a string of key saves against São Paulo at the Morumbi. In January, though, Valle made headlines for the wrong reasons, detained after failing a breathalyser test during a traffic stop in Quito. He served a 20-day sentence, paid a fine and was given a driving ban. LDU took disciplinary measures too, but supported him through it. He also won backing from Miguel Ángel Loor, president of the Ecuadorian league, who said: “He will surely come out of this problem soon and continue his career in an impeccable way, being who he is: the best keeper in the country.”

data.name: Moisés Caicedo
data.description: From Santo Domingo's dusty pitches to breaking the British transfer record, Caicedo is on quite a journey. When Chelsea paid Brighton £115m in 2023 they gave him an eight-year contract, a huge financial show of confidence in his ability. And, after what he called “a difficult start, I felt the pressure”, he has lived up to that billing at Stamford Bridge. A calm, sensitive character, his childhood nickname Niño Moi – the Boy Moi – has stuck, and he launched his Niño Moi 23 Academy in Guayas last year, saying he hoped it would “shape both sporting talents and exemplary citizens, grounded in good values”. Faith drives him: “I pray at every match, win or lose … I ask God to see us through each match without injuries.” He arrived at the World Cup carrying over a suspension following his red card in last year’s qualifier against Argentina, a match that began with his mum, Carmen, walking out to deliver the matchball to the referee.

data.name: Jeremy Arévalo
data.description: One of Ecuador’s most promising young strikers, combining physical power with technical quality and modern centre-forward intelligence. Born in Spain to Ecuadorian parents, Arévalo was developed by Racing Santander’s academy and made a £6.5m move to Stuttgart last January. With dual nationality, he played for Spain at under-18 level before committing his future to La Tri and making his debut last November against Canada. The Stuttgart board member Fabian Wohlgemuth said: “He already has a lot of attributes a striker needs, he’s still young and we see a lot of potential. We want to unlock it with him.” He was at the centre of controversy last year amid reports that Ecuador’s coach, Sebastián Beccacece, had left him out of a friendly after staff felt Arévalo had turned up to the camp overweight. Arévalo responded simply: “I am here to keep developing, step by step. I am incredibly proud of every minute I get to play with the Ecuadorian national team."

data.name: Jackson Porozo
data.description: Ecuador have some exceptional talent in the centre of defence. Willian Pacho and Piero Hincapié faced off in the final of the Champions League in May. But away from these decorated elite centre-backs, the Mexico-based Porozo gives his manager options. Called up aged 22 to his first World Cup in Qatar, he was drafted into Gustavo Alfaro’s starting XI for the second group game against the Netherlands as Ecuador switched from a back four to a back three. Porozo is a better fit than anyone else on the right of a back three. At 1.93m (6ft 4in), he’s also a big threat at set pieces and glanced in a free-kick in a warm-up friendly against Saudi Arabia. He has put his versatility down to playing football on the streets as a youngster. “I have the attributes to play in any position,” he said a few years ago. “My father always wanted me to be a footballer. Thanks to God, the opportunity has come to play professionally.”

data.name: Yaimar Medina
data.description: Medina’s first cap didn’t come in just any game – age 19, he came off the bench in Curitiba to make his international debut against Brazil. He was still playing for Independiente del Valle at the time and getting used to the idea of playing out of position – he started out as a left-winger. “I don’t feel like a guest, I feel like I’m part of the group and I’m here to earn my place,” Medina said before his debut. A few weeks after that baptism of fire – which ended in a 1-0 defeat – Medina was on his way to Europe, making the familiar first step among Ecuadorian players to Belgium and Genk. In a demonstration of the faith Sebastián Beccacece has in the left-back, the only other qualifier that Medina featured in was the return fixture against Brazil – when Ecuador kept a clean sheet in a goalless draw. Versatility and enthusiasm are the sort of qualities that have earned Medina a seat on the plane to North America.
